title: Midterms

jokes.text: There is no 'I' in freedom. Freedom is not 'medom.' It's 'wedom.'
jokes.setup: Furlong is giving a rousing campaign speech, riffing on the old cliché 'there's no I in team'
jokes.punchline: He invents the word 'wedom' as the opposite of 'medom,' neither of which are real words — the logic is completely nonsensical yet delivered with total conviction
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it's a perfect satirical riff on the empty rhetoric of political speeches. The escalating logic — taking a familiar cliché and inventing absurd portmanteau words — is sharp and character-specific. Furlong as a buffoon-congressman selling nonsense to a crowd is perfectly realized. The deadpan conviction amplifies it.

jokes.text: I fluffed 'em, now go fuck 'em.
jokes.setup: Furlong has just come offstage from his rousing speech to thunderous applause
jokes.punchline: He immediately drops the act and reduces the entire political moment to a crude sexual metaphor — 'fluffed' the crowd, now the party apparatus should close the deal
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best introduction of Furlong's character. In five words, it establishes him as a man who sees political performance as pure pornographic transaction, immediately code-switching from crowd-pleasing demagogue to cynical operator. The economy is extraordinary — the setup (entire speech) yields the punchline (five crude words). Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. Approaches the 8-anchor territory convincingly.

jokes.text: It's like a happy Nuremberg.
jokes.setup: Selina and a staffer observe Furlong's rally — the crowd is enormous, enthusiastic, unified
jokes.punchline: Selina compares the spectacle to a Nuremberg rally but qualifies it as 'happy' — the comparison to fascist pageantry is the dark joke, the word 'happy' is the deadpan softener that makes it land
jokes.explanation: Three words that do enormous work. The joke is in the juxtaposition — invoking the most infamous political rally in history to describe an American political event, then undercutting the horror with 'happy.' It's Veep's political satire at its sharpest: the show's thesis that American politics rhymes with historical fascism, delivered as a throwaway wisecrack. Genuinely exceptional economy.

jokes.text: Where'd the Red Sea go? That lady just parted it.
jokes.setup: A woman at the rally apparently walks through the crowd and the crowd parts for her
jokes.punchline: Biblical comparison to Moses parting the Red Sea, delivered as casual crowd commentary
jokes.explanation: Competent, mildly amusing throwaway line. The biblical comparison is well-worn territory but delivered with appropriate casualness. Functional crowd-scene humor.

jokes.text: Great, so much for the retired mobster vote.
jokes.setup: Ben is told they've lost Florida's third, fifth, and sixth districts
jokes.punchline: He reframes Florida's voter demographic as retired mobsters — a cynical joke about Florida's elderly population and corrupt political reputation
jokes.explanation: Sharp political wit — the joke conflates Florida's actual reputation (retirement communities + organized crime history) into a single caustic riff. Ben's deadpan delivery style makes it land well. Good economy, strong specificity.

jokes.text: Florida... ought to break it off at Jacksonville and row it to Cuba.
jokes.setup: Florida keeps delivering bad electoral news
jokes.punchline: Ben suggests literally detaching Florida from the US and sending it to Cuba — the absurdity of the geographical suggestion and the Cuba destination (Cold War enemy) amplifies the dismissal
jokes.explanation: A vivid, specific piece of political geographic absurdism. The Jacksonville detail makes it feel like real frustration rather than generic dismissal. Cuba as destination is a nice political dig. Ben's character (world-weary operative) is well-served.

jokes.text: Are you getting ready to dump a bucket of pig's blood on my head?
jokes.setup: Ben is getting increasingly bad news from staffers — Florida gone, West Coast bad, New England worse than worst projections
jokes.punchline: He compares the situation to the Carrie prom scene — all the good news has been a setup to humiliate him with pig's blood
jokes.explanation: A smart cultural reference that works because it accurately describes the escalating bad-news structure of the scene. Ben's paranoia is character-appropriate. The Carrie reference earns its place rather than being a cheap name-drop because the structural parallel is real.

jokes.text: Dale, I'm so sorry. What are we gonna do without you in the Senate? ... Yeah, you lost, Dale. Oh, my God. And, actually, I was told that you'd been told.
jokes.setup: Selina calls Dale offering condolences, treating it as a bereavement call
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Dale doesn't know he lost yet — Selina has been offering condolences to someone who thinks they might still win. Then the further embarrassment: 'I was told that you'd been told.'
jokes.explanation: A cringe masterpiece compressed into about 15 seconds. The structure is perfect: the false assumption (Dale knows), the dawning horror (he doesn't), the attempt to cover ('I was told you'd been told'), and Selina's immediate pivot to get off the phone. It's character-perfect — Selina's self-preservation instinct kicking in over genuine empathy. The line 'I was told that you'd been told' is a piece of bureaucratic ass-covering that's deeply funny on its own.

jokes.text: I want an expanded role in deficit reduction talks. I want a Cartier fucking dildo.
jokes.setup: Selina is listing her legitimate political demands — one-on-ones, infrastructure, education, deficit reduction
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of reasonable political ambitions suddenly terminates in a luxury sex toy. The specificity of 'Cartier' (a jeweler) is the key — it's not just a dildo, it's a status-symbol dildo
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalation structure is textbook perfect — four reasonable political demands that establish a pattern, then the total non sequitur that shatters it. The brand name does triple duty: it establishes class (Selina wants luxury goods), absurdity (Cartier doesn't make this), and character (even her frustration is status-conscious). The word 'fucking' is placed perfectly. This is comparable in quality to the 8-anchor example.

jokes.text: Amy, I'm so sorry. Is he speaking? Well, he told my mom he felt worse that time he ate gay Jap raw fish shit. Oh, Amy, that's a great sign, because that's a very complex sentence.
jokes.setup: Amy reveals her father may have had a stroke; Selina expresses concern and asks if he's speaking
jokes.punchline: Amy quotes her father's sentence — which is a complex, offensive string of slurs — and Selina identifies it as a neurological positive sign based on its grammatical complexity while ignoring its content entirely
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed multi-layer joke. Layer 1: Amy's father is a bigot, revealing her backstory with brutal efficiency. Layer 2: Selina's clinical analysis of the sentence as a positive cognitive sign is both medically somewhat accurate and morally oblivious. Layer 3: The audience winces at the content while laughing at Selina's utter indifference to it. Very characteristic of Veep's brand of cringe-dark comedy.

jokes.text: Unless he's slurring his words.
jokes.setup: Selina has just said the complex racist sentence is 'a great sign' neurologically
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts her own reassurance with the caveat that it only counts if he wasn't slurring — a callback to the stroke concern, delivered as a dry afterthought
jokes.explanation: A tight, economical button to the preceding joke. Selina's momentary comfort is immediately withdrawn. The joke earns its place as a structural beat that deepens the cringe.

jokes.text: So you got what I said before? Yes, Cartier dildo.
jokes.setup: Amy has been distracted by news of her father's stroke during Selina's list of demands
jokes.punchline: Amy confirms she absorbed the list — and the thing she retained was specifically the Cartier dildo, not any of the political demands
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback. The callback confirms that the most absurd item in the list was the most memorable, which validates the original joke's structure. The deadpan confirmation is its own comedic beat. Amy's professionalism applied to something ridiculous is very Veep.

jokes.text: I was thinking about my dad, but I am not now.
jokes.setup: Amy confirms she got the Cartier dildo message
jokes.punchline: Amy admits the absurdity of the demand has displaced her father's medical emergency from her mind — a perfect encapsulation of Veep's workplace dysfunction overriding normal human priorities
jokes.explanation: Eight words that nail the show's central theme: these people's professional obsessions crowd out basic humanity. Amy has just been told her father might be dying, yet a luxury sex toy demand has overridden it. The deadpan matter-of-factness makes it funnier than any dramatic delivery could. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Dana, Dana, Dana, Dana.
jokes.setup: Gary keeps bringing up his girlfriend Dana when they're trying to do crisis management
jokes.punchline: Sue imitates Gary's girlfriend-obsession with a droning repetition of the name, perfectly capturing his monomaniacal affection
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional character beat. Sue's impersonation via name repetition is economical and pointed. Gary's obliviousness to social context makes him a reliable comedy engine throughout the episode.

jokes.text: So when it hits 2:00 A.M., my eyes will say Holocaust, my mouth will say Carnival.
jokes.setup: Selina wants a specific lipstick (Miami Sunburst) for the long election night ahead
jokes.punchline: She describes the aesthetic split of exhausted eyes vs. bright lipstick as a Holocaust/Carnival contrast — the most extreme possible axis for a cosmetics decision
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's crown jewel. The joke operates on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) Selina's vanity expressed through geopolitical tragedy-scale language, (2) the specificity of Holocaust/Carnival as the precise aesthetic opposition, (3) the fact that she's describing her own face using these terms without any irony, (4) the political satire — an American VP casually invoking the Holocaust to discuss makeup. The character integration is perfect — only Selina would think this way. The economy is perfect. It approaches the 9.5 anchor example in its craft. Legitimately one of Veep's great lines.

jokes.text: Or why this fucking Internet is sketchier than our economic forecasts?
jokes.setup: Selina asks about Ohio results while frustrated with the slow Internet
jokes.punchline: She draws an equivalence between the unreliable Internet and the administration's unreliable economic forecasts — the self-own about their governance is disguised as a tech complaint
jokes.explanation: Sharp political self-awareness from Selina. The joke is in the equivalence — two sketchy, unreliable systems. Her willingness to compare government forecasts to a broken laptop is accidentally honest and funny.

jokes.text: Oh, that's 'cause your laptop is still running on Windows 2000 and shit.
jokes.setup: Selina complains about the Internet being sketchy
jokes.punchline: Jonah's casual, slightly contemptuous diagnosis — the VP of the United States is using prehistoric software — is funny both for the mundane IT explanation and the 'and shit' casualness
jokes.explanation: Functional background character joke. The bathos of the VP's tech problems is amusing. Jonah's casual disrespect is characteristic.

jokes.text: Tonight there's gonna be a plane crash shunting a train wreck onto a crowded highway.
jokes.setup: Jonah is dramatically setting up the evening's electoral disaster predictions
jokes.punchline: He escalates disaster metaphors — plane crash into train wreck into crowded highway — a cascading catastrophe image for political failure
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed escalating disaster metaphor. The image of one disaster causing another causing another is vivid and specific. Slightly undermined because disaster metaphors are common political comedy territory.

jokes.text: Why, you got money problems? Not money problems, money challenges.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks if Mike has money problems after Mike jokes about buying stock in the disaster
jokes.punchline: Mike corrects 'problems' to 'challenges' — the classic corporate euphemism for serious financial trouble, a self-help reframe that makes the situation funnier by minimizing it
jokes.explanation: A classic deflection joke — Mike's use of corporate euphemism to describe financial catastrophe is character-specific and economical. The beat timing (pause before 'money challenges') is crucial to the delivery.

jokes.text: Want to bet how long it takes Chung to mention his war record? No. 100 bucks says he drops it in the first five seconds.
jokes.setup: Danny Chung, a political rival, appears on TV — known for constantly invoking his military service
jokes.punchline: Selina says 'no' to the bet but then immediately offers the bet herself — she knows the answer so the bet isn't a bet, it's a demonstration
jokes.explanation: The setup is strong — the joke about politicians weaponizing military service is sharp — and the immediate payoff makes it satisfying.

jokes.text: And I intend to serve you the way I served the American people overseas when they needed me. [immediately] Oh, ker-chung, ker-ching. Pay up.
jokes.setup: Selina just bet 100 bucks that Chung mentions his war record in the first five seconds
jokes.punchline: He does so in literally his first sentence. Selina's 'ker-chung, ker-ching' is a perfect portmanteau of 'ker-ching' (cash register) and 'Chung' (his name) — a new coinage that works on two levels
jokes.explanation: The payoff is immediate and satisfying. But what elevates this from a simple callback is 'ker-chung, ker-ching' — Selina invents a portmanteau in real time that means both 'he did it' and 'I win money.' The wordplay is genuinely clever and the spontaneous joy of winning her own bet is character-specific. This ranks high for originality within the context of the episode.

jokes.text: Hey, we are ahead on voter turnout in Lake County, Indiana. J-dog out.
jokes.setup: Selina calls for something positive; the only good news is a minor voter turnout lead in one small county
jokes.punchline: Jonah delivers this micro-win with maximum swagger ('J-dog out'), signs off, and completely steals Selina's moment — she was about to announce the exact same thing
jokes.explanation: Jonah upstaging Selina is a perfectly constructed humiliation. He takes her material, delivers it first with unearned confidence, and exits dramatically on the weakest possible news. His obliviousness to the social hierarchy is Jonah's defining trait and this is it in miniature. The 'J-dog out' sign-off is both hilarious and deeply pathetic.

jokes.text: I was saying exactly that.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just announced the Lake County thing and left. Someone asks what Selina was about to say.
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat, defeated 'I was saying exactly that' — delivered to an empty room that didn't let her have the one good moment of the night
jokes.explanation: Four words of pure comedic defeat. The line works because of what surrounds it — the entire build-up of Selina being robbed of her one positive contribution to the night. The deadpan resignation is everything. Perfect button.

jokes.text: Yeah, that'll take the edge off this cornhole.
jokes.setup: Selina announces the Lake County lead as if it's meaningful
jokes.punchline: Ben dismisses the Lake County news as an absurdly insufficient salve for a catastrophic night, using 'cornhole' as a vulgar synonym for the political disaster they're experiencing
jokes.explanation: Ben's dark-comic relief function is well-served here. The contrast between 'Lake County' and 'cornhole' is appropriately obscene and specific.

jokes.text: Is this it? Nope, it's a rape alarm. Like she's ever gonna need that. I mean, she's not ugly, but she's got a lot of security.
jokes.setup: Mike is selling things on eBay during a crisis. He's identifying an item and commenting on it.
jokes.punchline: He dismisses Selina's need for a rape alarm with the specific reasoning: not because she's unattractive but because of her security detail. The precision of the logic (ruling out ugliness first, then arriving at security) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke's construction is notable — Mike's reasoning process is itself funny. He doesn't lead with security; he leads with appearance ('she's not ugly'), as if he considered and rejected that explanation before arriving at the correct one. The structure mimics genuine deliberation. The dark subject matter is handled with Veep's characteristic bluntness.

jokes.text: I have a cracked keel, I have to dry-dock it. There's a bilge, which I don't even know where the fuck...
jokes.setup: Mike bought a boat he can't afford and can't sell. He's trying to explain his financial crisis.
jokes.punchline: Mike's increasingly desperate nautical vocabulary — deploying terms he clearly half-understands ('I don't even know where the fuck') — makes the boat ownership feel like an absurd, self-inflicted wound
jokes.explanation: The boat subplot is a running gag seeded through this episode. Mike's mid-crisis eBay selling and his confused nautical vocabulary establish his buffoonery well. The sentence that ends with 'where the fuck' because he genuinely doesn't know the word he needs is a nice character detail.

jokes.text: Okay, hmm, what is the solution to your problem, Gary? Oh, use another lipstick. Wow, that was easy. What is the solution to my problem? I sell a kidney?
jokes.setup: Gary is upset about the missing lipstick; Mike is upset about his boat debt
jokes.punchline: Selina demonstrates that Gary's problem (lipstick) has a trivial solution (use another lipstick), then inverts the logic for Mike's financial crisis — the only equivalent solution would be to sell a kidney
jokes.explanation: A well-structured parallel joke. Selina's rapid problem-solving mode applied to wildly different-scale problems creates the comedy. The kidney escalation is dark but proportionate. The 'Wow, that was easy' self-congratulation before the contrast is structurally sound.

jokes.text: You know what I'd like? I'd like to be cryogenically suspended. Yeah, and then be woken up in the future? No, never wake up. Just stay suspended.
jokes.setup: Ben is hiding in a back room, overwhelmed by the election disaster
jokes.punchline: The common cliché is cryogenic suspension to be woken in the future. Ben subverts it — he doesn't want to be woken up. Ever. It's a suicidal ideation joke dressed as a science fiction whimsy.
jokes.explanation: A superbly constructed dark joke. The setup uses the familiar two-beat structure ('cryogenically suspended... to be woken up in the future?') then the subversion ('No, never wake up') is perfectly timed. The joke is both genuinely funny and a precise character statement about Ben's exhausted nihilism. The simplicity of 'No, never wake up. Just stay suspended' is brilliant in its quietness. Comparable to the 8-anchor example.

jokes.text: Are you drunk? No, I'm just depressed.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if Ben is drunk after his cryogenics wish
jokes.punchline: Ben clarifies he's not drunk — he's just clinically depressed. Drunk would be a better explanation.
jokes.explanation: A tight exchange that works on Ben's weary deadpan. Preferring to be identified as drunk rather than depressed would be the socially safer answer; his honesty is both funny and slightly sad.

jokes.text: He made us go river rafting together. Catherine got giardia. And I had to listen to Andrew bang that skank on the riverbank all night.
jokes.setup: Selina is explaining why she doesn't trust Kent Davison — he made her fake-reconcile with her ex-husband for a photo op
jokes.punchline: The escalating consequences of the rafting trip: (1) daughter gets parasites, (2) ex-husband has sex with another woman loudly on the riverbank during what's supposed to be a family healing moment
jokes.explanation: A miniature escalation sequence within a longer speech. The two elements — Catherine's giardia and Andrew's infidelity — are both humiliating in different registers (motherhood, marriage) and together paint a picture of comprehensive degradation in the name of a political photo op. The word 'skank' and 'riverbank' both do characterization work.

jokes.text: I said to Kent, 'Can you make me feel good about this somehow?' And you know what he does? He points to a graph.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Kent to help her emotionally process a humiliating experience
jokes.punchline: His response to a human emotional plea is to point to a graph. The complete emotional register mismatch is the joke — Kent's pure data-brain encountering a feelings-based request
jokes.explanation: Kent is perfectly characterized before he even appears on screen. The joke is economical and specific — not just 'he showed me data' but 'he points to a graph.' The physical precision of pointing to a graph in response to an emotional plea is exactly right. High character integration for Kent.

jokes.text: He is cold. Yeah, he's got ice in his semen.
jokes.setup: Selina says Kent is cold; Ben agrees
jokes.punchline: Ben escalates 'cold' from personality descriptor to biological fact — the ice-semen image is startling, specific, and visual in a deeply unpleasant way that somehow works
jokes.explanation: A memorable, slightly shocking character description that escalates the 'cold' metaphor to its logical extreme. The joke is in the specificity — not 'he's like ice' but the visceral biological image. Ben's capacity for inspired grotesquerie is on display. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: He's already squirreled away in an air vent somewhere with his fucking statistics.
jokes.setup: Selina asks when Kent is coming back; Ben says he's already here
jokes.punchline: The image of Kent in an air vent with statistics — part rodent, part spy, living inside the building's infrastructure — captures his secretive, omnipresent quality
jokes.explanation: The air vent image is sharp and specific character description. 'Squirreled away' is a perfect verb choice — it makes Kent simultaneously small and hoarding. Combined with the statistics, it completes a portrait of a data-obsessed creature living in the building's walls.

jokes.text: You know that portrait with the guy who looks like a fat Wolverine? It's just one door on the left from there.
jokes.setup: Selina asks where Kent's office is
jokes.punchline: Ben gives directions using a portrait of a fat Wolverine lookalike as a landmark — a deeply specific, bureaucratic detail about the building's art collection that's funnier for being totally plausible in a government building
jokes.explanation: The visual joke works because it's a specific and plausible detail in a government building — official portraits of minor historical figures who happen to look like superheroes. Also a setup for a callback later.

jokes.text: My good friend POTUS, my Gamma Chi brother, is gonna summon me to his office and he's gonna show me a sword and he's gonna tell me to take the sword and slide it down my throat until it comes out my ass.
jokes.setup: Ben warns that the political consequences of losing the House will fall on him
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'show me a sword' to the increasingly specific route of the sword is grotesque and precise — the detail about where it 'comes out' transforms a standard political threat into something viscerally medieval
jokes.explanation: Ben's political anxieties expressed as medieval torture imagery is very Veep. The specificity of the sword's trajectory — down the throat until it exits the other end — is grotesque escalation at its best. The 'my Gamma Chi brother' detail of POTUS is a nice character note about their hollow relationship.

jokes.text: Ben, one day we are gonna laugh about this.
jokes.setup: Ben has just described being politically destroyed with a sword going through him
jokes.punchline: Selina's hollow comfort — 'we're gonna laugh about this' — is a complete social platitude in response to a genuine catastrophe. The banality of the comfort is funny against the horror of what Ben described.
jokes.explanation: Competent button to Ben's speech. The contrast between the violent imagery and Selina's cheerful platitude is the joke. Works adequately without being particularly inspired.

jokes.text: although the vice president appreciates your offer of stale pastries and pleasant conversation, she will not be doing the morning shows tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Sue is shutting down morning show requests over the phone with maximum disdain
jokes.punchline: The mock-formal language ('the vice president appreciates your offer') applied to describe morning show booking as 'stale pastries and pleasant conversation' — reducing the entire media apparatus to a dreary social obligation
jokes.explanation: Sue's telephone gatekeeping persona is always a source of pleasure in Veep. The formal language ('appreciates your offer') contrasted with the contemptuous characterization of what's being offered is characteristic of her. 'Stale pastries and pleasant conversation' is a lovely dismissal of morning TV culture.

jokes.text: Is that a crack about my boat? Really funny. Put your boat on eBay. Can you put a sinking ship on eBay? You can put anything on there.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks if Selina is 'deserting a sinking ship' by skipping morning shows; Mike takes offense
jokes.punchline: Mike takes the political metaphor literally, treating 'sinking ship' as a reference to his actual boat. Then the reporter and Sue riff on putting a sinking ship on eBay, which doubles back on Mike's eBay plan.
jokes.explanation: The boat subplot pays off beautifully here. A political metaphor ('sinking ship') accidentally hitting Mike's literal situation is a solid structural joke. The riffing exchange between Sue and the reporter is rapid and economical.

jokes.text: Isn't that the catchphrase of the world's creepiest babysitter?
jokes.setup: Furlong on TV says 'I think there's gonna be quite a few surprises tonight'
jokes.punchline: Dan reframes Furlong's political promise as something a predatory babysitter would say — the same words work as either empty political theater or sinister domestic threat
jokes.explanation: A sharp reframing joke. The key insight is that political rhetoric is often indistinguishable from sinister small-talk. The 'world's creepiest babysitter' is a vivid image. Good economy — one clause does all the work.

jokes.text: Kent Davison? The Pol Pot of pie charts.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions Kent Davison is back; a reporter reacts
jokes.punchline: Comparing Kent to Pol Pot (Khmer Rouge dictator) applied specifically to the domain of pie charts — the escalation from 'ruthless political operator' to 'genocide leader' applied to data visualization is the joke
jokes.explanation: Five words that are a masterclass in comedic compression. 'Pol Pot of pie charts' is a perfect juxtaposition — the most extreme historical monster available applied to the most boring corporate tool. The joke works because it's both a comment on Kent's ruthlessness AND a perfect skewering of how seriously DC insiders take data-driven politics. Highly quotable. Approaches the 8-anchor in quality.

jokes.text: Wow, that guy is ruthless. A total inspiration.
jokes.setup: Someone says Kent Davison is ruthless — this should be a criticism
jokes.punchline: Dan agrees and immediately frames ruthlessness as aspirational ('a total inspiration') — revealing that Dan's values are completely inverted from normal human ethics
jokes.explanation: A concise Dan character beat. Ruthlessness-as-inspiration is exactly Dan's worldview. The joke is in the framing flip — the same quality that disturbs others is what Dan wants to emulate. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Wipe that thing off before you hand it to me.
jokes.setup: An assistant hands Furlong the phone; Furlong recoils
jokes.punchline: Furlong's refusal to touch Dan Egan's phone without it being wiped first — treating his own assistant as contaminated — is both a power play and an expression of pure contempt
jokes.explanation: A quick character establishment beat for Furlong's contemptuous management style. The germaphobe detail makes him slightly more vivid.

jokes.text: Hey there, pretty boy. You know what they call guys like you in prison? Sweetmeat.
jokes.setup: Furlong calls Dan and immediately opens with a sexual threat
jokes.punchline: 'Sweetmeat' as a prison nickname — Furlong implies Dan would be a target for sexual violence as both an insult and a power flex. The fake-friendly 'hey there, pretty boy' opener makes it more sinister.
jokes.explanation: Furlong's specific brand of homophobic-adjacent sexual threatening is well-established here. The word 'Sweetmeat' is both archaic and threatening. Character-specific to Furlong's style of intimidation.

jokes.text: Nobody plans on going to prison, dipshit. That's just the way it works out sometimes.
jokes.setup: Dan says he has no plan to go to prison; Furlong responds
jokes.punchline: Furlong's 'nobody plans on it' — delivered as life wisdom from someone with obvious experience of this reality — suggests he has personal familiarity with unplanned incarceration
jokes.explanation: The joke works because 'that's just the way it works out sometimes' sounds like folk wisdom from personal experience. The implication that Furlong knows prison from the inside is never stated but strongly implied, which is good comedic restraint.

jokes.text: you're gonna have to be prepared to be gay for the stay 'cause you're going down. Chin up, buddy. You'll be fine. Mouth open.
jokes.setup: Furlong is threatening Dan with investigation if he loses and returns to the Oversight Committee
jokes.punchline: He escalates the prison rape threat, then gives mock-encouraging sign-off ('Chin up, buddy. You'll be fine.') before the devastating kicker 'Mouth open' — which retroactively makes 'chin up' and 'mouth open' into physical directives that form a sequence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best-constructed jokes. The structure is exceptional: the prison threat, then the reversal into mock-kindness ('Chin up, buddy'), then the devastating kicker 'Mouth open' which works on two levels simultaneously — it's both a continuation of the sexual threat AND a phone-call sign-off gesture. The fact that 'Chin up / Mouth open' form a paired physical instruction is a masterwork of construction. It's deeply character-specific to Furlong. Approaches the 9.5 anchor in structure.

jokes.text: Fat Wolverine. [title card/reaction beat as Selina walks past the portrait]
jokes.setup: Ben described Kent's office location as 'one door left from the portrait of the guy who looks like a fat Wolverine'
jokes.punchline: Selina walks past the portrait and we (or she) identifies it — the callback confirms Ben's description was accurate and the portrait exists
jokes.explanation: A satisfying visual callback paid off in two words. The rewatch bonus is high — audiences who caught Ben's description get the reward. The economy is perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, Kent. Look at you, you're all back. I see they took out the sink and the toilet. Made it a little roomier for you.
jokes.setup: Selina enters Kent's tiny office to reconnect
jokes.punchline: She implies his previous office was so small it had plumbing fixtures installed — it was essentially a converted bathroom. The cheerful delivery ('made it a little roomier') is the deadpan kicker.
jokes.explanation: A vivid, specific insult that works as both a joke about Washington hierarchy (small offices = low status) and a character note about Kent's institutional invisibility. The cheerful framing makes it cut more.

jokes.text: Am I supposed to ask a question now? Oh, sure. If you want. Fire away. And what would that question be? Well, that question might be what do you think is the best reposition to take? And what would the answer to that question be? And the answer would be standing right in front of you.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to get Kent to recognize her value; she speaks in elliptical non-statements; Kent matches her evasiveness
jokes.punchline: The entire exchange is a nested series of meta-questions — 'Am I supposed to ask?', 'What would the question be?', 'What would the answer be?' — a bureaucratic hall-of-mirrors where neither side says anything directly
jokes.explanation: A sustained comedic sequence about political indirection. Both characters speak entirely in meta-language — questions about questions, answers about answers — because neither can make a direct claim. The structure escalates beautifully into Selina finally just pointing to herself as 'the answer.' The scene satirizes how political actors constantly telegraph intentions without ever stating them. Structurally impressive.

jokes.text: I don't think I was actually very clear before. / No.
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered an elaborate indirect pitch for her own elevation.
jokes.punchline: She admits she wasn't clear; Kent's single-word confirmation ('No') is the deadpan punchline — no softening, no elaboration, just agreement.
jokes.explanation: Kent's one-word 'No.' is a masterclass in deadpan economy. The lack of any attempt to soften or contextualize his agreement is perfectly character-specific — Kent doesn't do diplomacy. The economy score is maximal. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: My uncle used to have this saying. He used to say to me, 'Honey, if you're not at the table, you're probably on the menu.' And, Kent, I will not be eaten.
jokes.setup: Selina realizes her previous meeting with Kent was unclear and tries again with a metaphor
jokes.punchline: She delivers what's meant to be a powerful political aphorism, then adds 'I will not be eaten' — the literal extension of the dining metaphor, which makes it unintentionally absurd
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Selina's rhetorical overreach — the proverb is standard political wisdom, but 'I will not be eaten' takes the metaphor too literally, slightly deflating her intended power move. Kent's subsequent 'Why would anyone want to eat you?' sharpens it.

jokes.text: Why would anyone want to eat you?
jokes.setup: Selina declares 'I will not be eaten' as a statement of political self-assertion
jokes.punchline: Kent takes it literally — not as a challenge to her political position but as a factual question about cannibalism — his data-brain refusing to process metaphor
jokes.explanation: Six words of perfect Kent characterization. His literal interpretation of Selina's metaphor is both a character joke AND a deflation of her grandiosity — two things at once. The comedy is entirely in Kent's complete absence of metaphorical thinking. His deadpan delivery of this question, as if it's a genuine inquiry, is exceptional character comedy. High character integration — only Kent could deliver this line without it being played as comedy.

jokes.text: You do know that I am not running for office right now? I am, in fact, the vice president. I wouldn't dispute your title. I might question your role.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to establish her relevance to Kent
jokes.punchline: Kent's response distinguishes 'title' (VP) from 'role' — implying the title is real but the role is negligible. The distinction is precise, clinical, and devastating.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. 'I wouldn't dispute your title. I might question your role.' is devastating in its precision. It acknowledges her position while completely hollowing it out. The distinction between 'title' and 'role' is both a joke and the show's central thesis about the VP's powerlessness. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific to Kent's surgical politeness. One of the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: It would be great if you would stand up when the vice president enters a room. Oh, Lord, all right. Oh, Lord. There you go. Oh, at ease, Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina, stung by Kent's dismissal, demands the formal gesture of him standing when she enters
jokes.punchline: Kent's theatrical 'Oh, Lord' — he's not disrespectful enough to refuse but makes clear this is absurd — followed by Selina's anxious 'at ease, Kent' because the formal standing is making her uncomfortable too
jokes.explanation: A nice cringe beat where Selina wins the tactical battle (he stands) but loses the war (it's embarrassing). Her quick 'at ease, Kent' reveals that she wanted the gesture as power, not as an actual protocol.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's the only thing I'd save if this building were on fire.
jokes.setup: Kent shows Selina a large mug he uses for coffee; she reacts with surprise at its size
jokes.punchline: Kent declares his mug as his most valued possession — the thing worth saving from a burning building. The joke is the complete inversion of expected values from a powerful political strategist.
jokes.explanation: Kent's emotional attachment to an enormous mug while being emotionally detached from everything else is a good character detail. The mundane nature of his affection is funny against his cold-operator persona.

jokes.text: Everyone, POTUS has gone to bed. So let's make sure we make his dreams come true tonight by fighting until that last bullet.
jokes.setup: Selina learns POTUS is asleep on election night and decides to rally the troops
jokes.punchline: The speech is a parody of inspirational military rhetoric ('fight until that last bullet') used to motivate people to do work for a president who is literally asleep. The motivational frame completely undercuts itself.
jokes.explanation: Functional political satire. The image of fighting 'that last bullet' for a president who went to bed is a good irony. Character-appropriate for Selina trying to seem presidential in his absence.

jokes.text: Why don't you take the first bullet and put it through your brain?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Ben to 'fight until that last bullet'
jokes.punchline: Ben reroutes the bullet metaphor — instead of fighting for POTUS, use it on yourself as a form of merciful exit from the situation
jokes.explanation: A very sharp callback. Ben takes Selina's motivational military metaphor and immediately redirects it as a suicide joke — not out of genuine despair but as a commentary on how useless the motivational speech is. The economy is excellent. 'First bullet / your brain' vs. 'last bullet / fight' is a clean structural subversion.

jokes.text: I'm gonna have more of a role in this administration. Well, I just crunched the numbers. I know. You're the numbers cruncher. And right now they taste pretty bland to me. Well, salt and pepper 'em.
jokes.setup: Selina demands a bigger role; Kent says he crunched the numbers
jokes.punchline: Selina pushes back ('they taste pretty bland') — using a food metaphor for data — and Kent's response ('salt and pepper 'em') continues the metaphor straight, suggesting the data are correct but can be flavored to her liking
jokes.explanation: The food/data extended metaphor is a nice exchange. Selina's 'they taste bland' is a good line; Kent's 'salt and pepper 'em' suggests he can adjust the presentation of the numbers without changing the underlying reality — a subtle joke about political spin.

jokes.text: Marion, listen very closely. You have as much chance of getting the vice president on your show as you have of getting your husband to leave that cheerleader.
jokes.setup: A morning show producer named Marion is pressing for an interview
jokes.punchline: Sue's metaphorical refusal uses Marion's apparently known personal situation — her husband is having an affair with a cheerleader — as the benchmark of impossibility. Deeply personal, completely inappropriate, perfectly Sue.
jokes.explanation: Sue's telephone manner is perhaps the most consistent comedy engine in Veep. This is her at peak form — weaponizing personal knowledge of the caller's private life as a benchmark of impossibility. The specificity of 'cheerleader' (not just 'affair') is exactly the level of detail Sue would know and deploy. Her voice stays absolutely flat throughout. A masterclass in character-driven insult comedy.

jokes.text: Hey, dumpling. It's Gary. I'm sorry I missed you. It's just I can't find her lipstick and I thought maybe it fell out of the Leviathan at home.
jokes.setup: Gary calls his girlfriend Dana during a national crisis to ask about lipstick
jokes.punchline: Gary calls his girlfriend 'dumpling,' then asks her to check for the VP's lipstick in 'the Leviathan' — apparently the name of something at home (a bag, a purse) — the diminutive pet name against the Leviathan's biblical/epic name is funny
jokes.explanation: Gary's total conflation of his personal and professional life is on full display — calling his girlfriend 'dumpling' while on crisis duty to ask about lipstick. 'The Leviathan' is a good detail — naming a household item something epic is very Gary.

jokes.text: You can't reason with him. It'd be like explaining Supertramp to a Komodo dragon.
jokes.setup: They need to get Kent Davison on board with Selina's co-POTAL ambitions; Mike says you can't reason with him
jokes.punchline: The specific comparators — Supertramp (70s prog-rock band) to a Komodo dragon — are so wildly mismatched in type that the analogy works by being completely useless as an analogy while perfectly capturing the communication gap
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most genuinely original jokes. The specific pairing is crucial — not 'jazz to a dog' or 'opera to a fish' but Supertramp specifically (a very niche 70s band) and a Komodo dragon specifically (not a generic reptile but a venomous apex predator). The specificity makes it feel like a real thought rather than a formula. Selina's response ('I don't know what those words mean') is the perfect button — she genuinely doesn't process either the meaning OR the reference.

jokes.text: I don't know what those words mean.
jokes.setup: Mike uses the Supertramp/Komodo dragon analogy
jokes.punchline: Selina doesn't know if he's referring to the content of the analogy OR the actual words Supertramp/Komodo dragon — a perfectly calibrated response that works either way
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff to the Supertramp joke. Selina's line is funny in two ways: (1) she might genuinely not know what a Komodo dragon is, which would be character-appropriate, or (2) she's saying the analogy itself is meaningless. Both readings are funny and neither is ruled out.

jokes.text: I have a very strong feeling that Kent is gonna get in between me and POTUS, like some sort of thick rubber condom, and I have got to have... Unprotected. Unprotected access to the Oval Office.
jokes.setup: Selina describes her fear that Kent will block her access to the president
jokes.punchline: She deploys a condom metaphor, pauses before the word 'access' — giving Dan the perfect beat to drop 'unprotected' before she finishes, which Selina then incorporates as if it was her own word
jokes.explanation: A beautiful two-person joke construction. The setup requires Selina to choose a sexual metaphor, pause, and let Dan complete it — which she then adopts as the correct word. The structure is perfect: the condom setup, the pause, Dan's 'Unprotected,' and Selina's earnest repetition that confirms it. The political meaning ('unprotected access to the Oval') layered over the sexual metaphor makes it work on both levels.

jokes.text: You have to go and see your dad. This is the midterms. Amy, it's the fucking midterms. Go.
jokes.setup: Amy's dad is potentially dying; Selina spent the episode keeping Amy at work
jokes.punchline: After using the midterms as a reason for Amy to stay, Selina now uses the midterms as a reason for Amy to go — because they've just lost the House and Selina wants her gone. The same reasoning serving opposite ends.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character joke. The humor comes entirely from recognizing that Selina's logic is purely self-serving — she kept Amy using 'it's the midterms' and now dismisses her with the same phrase. The callback reveals Selina's full moral picture. The brutal pivot ('Go.') after invoking the same justification is sharp. High character integration.

jokes.text: Okay, everybody, it's official. We have lost... We have just lost the House. So put everything on the wagons and shoot the dogs because we have just lost the fucking House.
jokes.setup: The House has been lost; Ben makes the official announcement
jokes.punchline: Ben deploys pioneer/Western retreat imagery — 'put everything on the wagons and shoot the dogs' — as a metaphor for political catastrophe. The specificity of shooting the dogs (a real historical emergency procedure) makes the absurdity land harder.
jokes.explanation: Ben's announcement of political catastrophe through frontier retreat metaphor is both vivid and specific. 'Shoot the dogs' is the surprising dark element — the pioneer detail of killing the dogs before abandonment adds genuine darkness to the comedy. Character-appropriate for Ben's apocalyptic sensibility.

jokes.text: [What he just said. Yes!] — someone enthusiastically endorsing losing the House
jokes.setup: Ben dramatically announces they've lost the House
jokes.punchline: An unknown staffer apparently enthusiastically endorses the 'shoot the dogs' speech — the reaction beat suggests someone has been waiting all night for permission to give up
jokes.explanation: Background reaction beat. Functional but underdeveloped.

jokes.text: I'm gonna go pee pee and then we're gonna neutralize Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina rallies the team to take on Kent Davison as a political move
jokes.punchline: The gravitas of 'we're gonna neutralize Kent' is completely undercut by 'I'm gonna go pee pee first' — the childish bathroom language in a political strategy meeting is the joke
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the Machiavellian declaration ('neutralize Kent') and the infantile bathroom language ('pee pee') is sharp and character-specific. Selina's mixture of strategic ambition and casual informality is her defining quality, and this compresses it perfectly. 'Pee pee' is funnier than any more euphemistic choice would be.

jokes.text: Hey, Mike. Give me a hug. Uncle Ben's on his way out.
jokes.setup: The House has been lost; Ben approaches Mike
jokes.punchline: Ben positions himself as a doomed man — 'Uncle Ben' seeking a final hug before his political execution. The 'Uncle Ben' self-reference (rice brand) is a bonus.
jokes.explanation: Ben's self-aware political fatalism is his dominant character note. The 'Uncle Ben' framing is gentle and sad in a way that makes it funnier. His vulnerability in asking for a hug is unexpectedly touching and therefore funny.

jokes.text: What is that, Ralph Lauren? For men. Well, that's a good scent.
jokes.setup: Ben hugs Mike and notices his cologne
jokes.punchline: During what's meant to be an emotional farewell hug, they have a completely mundane cologne discussion. The total incongruity between the emotional situation and the perfume review is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A nice bathos beat. The pivot from doom to cologne appreciation is the joke. Works as a brief character-humanizing moment for Ben.

jokes.text: Hey, Brokeback Egan.
jokes.setup: Furlong runs into Dan after losing his race
jokes.punchline: Furlong's greeting doubles down on the prison/gay-threat subtext of the earlier call — calling Dan 'Brokeback Egan' connects to the film's gay cowboy theme, a continuation of his sustained homophobic threatening
jokes.explanation: A callback to Furlong's sustained harassment of Dan. The 'Brokeback' reference continues the homophobic taunt thread. Character-consistent but not as sharp as the 'Mouth open' joke it follows.

jokes.text: Screw you and the face you rode in on, Dan.
jokes.setup: Dan offers condolences on Furlong losing his race
jokes.punchline: Furlong modifies the idiom 'screw you and the horse you rode in on' — replacing 'horse' with 'face,' making it both a personal insult to Dan's appearance and a mangling of a familiar phrase
jokes.explanation: A genuine piece of linguistic invention. Replacing 'horse' with 'face' is unexpected and oddly effective — 'the face you rode in on' is both an insult and an appropriately bizarre image. The phrase modification is distinctively Furlong (he did the same thing with freedom/medom/wedom). It might be his verbal tic. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Couldn't have put that better myself, sir.
jokes.setup: Furlong has just incoherently reasoned that losing the House is bad for the country but good for him because it gets him off Dan's case — asking 'is that okay with you?'
jokes.punchline: Dan's deadpan 'Couldn't have put that better myself, sir' is pure sycophantic self-preservation — validating completely illogical reasoning rather than engaging with it
jokes.explanation: Dan's political survival instinct expressed in a single polished phrase. The absolute insincerity of agreeing with Furlong's jumbled reasoning is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Are you eating my pizza? No, sir, you... because you said that... No, I said don't wave it in my face. I didn't say eat it. It's still good. Eat it! Eat it all right now!
jokes.setup: Furlong's aide Will apparently ate Furlong's pizza after Furlong told him not to wave it in his face
jokes.punchline: The escalation from accusation to indignation to Furlong angrily commanding Will to eat the entire pizza right now as punishment. The loop — forbidden pizza becomes mandatory pizza — is perfect cringe logic.
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of rapid escalation. Will's confusion ('you said...') reveals the Kafkaesque nature of working for Furlong — instructions that can only be violated. But the coup de grâce is the punishment: Furlong doesn't take the pizza back, he commands Will to eat all of it RIGHT NOW. The reversal of the original prohibition into a forced consumption is structurally perfect. Furlong's rage as power expression is the show at its best.

jokes.text: Got here as soon as I could. Just really glad you could fit your father into your busy schedule. She means hi, honey.
jokes.setup: Amy arrives at the hospital where her father may be dying
jokes.punchline: Her sister's greeting is a devastating, resentment-laden barb. The mother's immediate translation ('she means hi, honey') suggests this is a well-worn family dynamic — the sister always does this and the mother always covers.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat family dysfunction comedy compressed brilliantly. Attack / Translation is a classic structure, but the mother's weary 'she means hi, honey' implies decades of the same dynamic. The audience laughs at recognition — everyone has this family. Works on a different register than the political comedy but lands just as well.

jokes.text: You said Dad was dying. Well, I'm so sorry to disappoint you, Amy.
jokes.setup: Amy apparently rushed to the hospital expecting her father to be dying
jokes.punchline: Sophie frames the father's survival as a disappointment to Amy — the most uncharitable possible interpretation of Amy's presence, delivered with mock apology
jokes.explanation: Sophie's attack is clever — she weaponizes Amy's reasonable concern as evidence of ill-intent. The 'I'm so sorry to disappoint you' mock-apology is pointed. Good cringe family comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Amy. You work for the vice president. It's not like it's Google.
jokes.setup: Amy's family makes her feel guilty about being absent; Sophie comments on the significance of Amy's job
jokes.punchline: Sophie dismisses 'working for the Vice President' as less prestigious than working for Google — a perfect distillation of modern status values where tech companies have displaced political office in cultural prestige
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest observational jokes. The inversion — VP as less impressive than tech company — is a real cultural insight delivered as a casual barb from a jealous sister. It works as family comedy, as political satire (about the diminished prestige of the VP's office), and as Silicon Valley commentary. Three registers simultaneously. The delivery's casual dismissiveness is key.

jokes.text: My pee pee is done. Let's go crack Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina announced she was going to 'pee pee' before neutralizing Kent
jokes.punchline: She returns with the same childish bathroom language, paired immediately with the Machiavellian 'crack Kent' — the whiplash between infantile and strategic is the joke
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces the joke's character observation. The juxtaposition holds up on second delivery. Short, effective.

jokes.text: Oh, God. It's fucking Big Bird.
jokes.setup: Selina and Dan round a corner and encounter Jonah unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Selina's reaction to Jonah's physical presence — he's extremely tall — is to call him 'Big Bird,' invoking the Sesame Street character. The register (children's TV character) applied to an adult political staffer is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A good Jonah physical comedy beat. 'Big Bird' works as both a height joke and a maturity joke — Big Bird is also naive and eager to please, which matches Jonah's personality.

jokes.text: You're great news. You even aced POTUS. You're like Neo. What's a Neo? He's from 'The Matrix.' Everything he does is awesome. The first movie. The sequels sucked. Guys, we agreed to let 'The Matrix' debate lie.
jokes.setup: Jonah tells Selina her campaign visits outperformed POTUS; his team celebrates her using Matrix references
jokes.punchline: Selina doesn't know what Neo is; a team member explains The Matrix; the team then has an immediate internal debate about the quality of The Matrix sequels that clearly has been ongoing — ending in a rule about not discussing it
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully observed political-nerd comedy moment. The stats team debate about Matrix sequels during an election crisis is both character-specific (these are tech/stats young men) and structurally funny — the VP's visit devolves instantly into their pre-existing argument. 'We agreed to let the Matrix debate lie' implies this argument has happened before.

jokes.text: Jesus, I can feel my virginity growing back in here.
jokes.setup: Selina observes Jonah's stats team — a room full of young male data nerds debating The Matrix
jokes.punchline: She characterizes the room's nerd energy as so anti-sexual that it's restorative of virginity — a creative and vivid expression of contempt
jokes.explanation: A fresh and memorable line. The specific construction — not 'this place is so unsexy' but the phenomenon of virginity GROWING BACK — is inventive and slightly disgusting in a way that works. It's specific to Selina's character (she is comfortable making sexual observations) and to the context (a room of socially stunted young men). Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Jesus, I can feel my virginity growing back in here.
jokes.setup: Dan is in Jonah's stats nerd room watching them debate The Matrix
jokes.punchline: Being surrounded by this level of geekery is making Dan feel virginal by osmosis
jokes.explanation: Instantly quotable, very Dan. 'I can feel my virginity growing back in here' is a vivid and original expression of contempt for nerd culture — framed not as intellectual disgust but as a physical/sexual phenomenon. The specificity of 'growing back' (rather than 'returning') implies a biological process. High quotability.

jokes.text: So what format do you want this in? Excel? Pie charts? Just, like, in English. Is that a racist joke? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Dan asks Jonah for the data in English; Jonah responds with 'Is that a racist joke?'
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'Yeah' — completely playing along with the absurd suggestion — is funnier than any denial would be. The exchange works because 'in English' could theoretically be interpreted as a slight against someone for whom English isn't a first language, but Jonah's paranoia in asking AND Dan's casual confirmation both reveal their characters
jokes.explanation: A rapid-fire exchange that works on two levels: Jonah's absurd hypersensitivity (looking for offense in 'in English') and Dan's complete indifference to the accusation ('Yeah.'). Dan's casual confirmation is the unexpected punchline — he just accepts the characterization and moves on. Sharp and economical.

jokes.text: Listen, Amy. Something has happened to the vice president. I know your dad is dying and I'm really, really sorry, Amy, but I think Dana took Selina's lipstick.
jokes.setup: Gary calls Amy at the hospital where her father may be dying, opening with dramatic urgency
jokes.punchline: The 'something happened to the VP' setup implies a political or physical crisis; the reveal that the emergency is lipstick theft — placed in explicit contrast with her father's potential death ('I know your dad is dying') — is the escalation-then-bathetic-deflation
jokes.explanation: The lipstick running gag meets the Amy's-father subplot in its most devastating form. Gary's structure is perfect — he opens with crisis language ('something happened'), explicitly acknowledges her father is DYING, then immediately pivots to lipstick. The explicit acknowledgment ('I know your dad is dying and I'm really, really sorry, BUT') is what makes it exceptional rather than just a running gag payoff. He knows it's wrong and does it anyway. This is Gary's character at its most complete.

jokes.text: Hope your boss at CVS is okay with you taking time off. Always with the last word. That's why you're single. Guys hate that. You have three kids by two different guys. Maybe your last word should have been no.
jokes.setup: Sophie dismisses Amy's job as barely better than a CVS pharmacy job
jokes.punchline: Amy escalates — Sophie mocks Amy for being single; Amy responds with 'you have three kids by two different guys' — Amy's comeback is more devastating, implying Sophie should have said 'no' to something (sex, relationship) rather than always saying things
jokes.explanation: Amy's comeback is sharper than Sophie's attack — the 'three kids by two different guys' stat is devastating, and the 'maybe your last word should have been no' is a well-constructed callback to Sophie's 'always with the last word' critique. Amy wins this exchange on points. The structure (attack / better counter-attack) is cleanly executed.

jokes.text: Kent. Where's the president? Asleep. Well, should you be in here? People need to think he's in here leading. But he's not. He is, according to the rumor I put out.
jokes.setup: Selina finds Kent in what she thinks is the Oval Office; he says POTUS is asleep
jokes.punchline: Kent has spread a rumor that POTUS is awake and leading — despite literally being the one who told everyone he went to bed. The joke is his casual acknowledgment of manufacturing reality.
jokes.explanation: A sharp commentary on political image management. Kent is manufacturing the president's presence while everyone knows he's asleep. His nonchalance about this is characteristically cold.

jokes.text: Come on, you are the vice president. By definition, you should be at half the height of the president.
jokes.setup: Selina argues she should be first in and last out of meetings with POTUS; Kent resists
jokes.punchline: Kent makes a literalist argument — 'vice' means half, therefore the VP should be at half the height. It's simultaneously a data-nerd argument AND a perfect metaphorical summary of the VP's constitutional irrelevance
jokes.explanation: A brilliant Kent line. The 'vice = half' argument is mock-logical but contains a real political truth — the show's entire premise compressed into one sentence. Kent's character is perfectly served: he would make a literalist definitional argument. The image of Selina at half-height is vivid and humiliating. Approaches the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Mm-hmm. You want to see where you are? Hi, Kent. How's it going? Thank you so much for bringing us the memo on aggregates. Now why don't you just go and fuck yourself in your own asshole?
jokes.setup: Kent tells Selina she should be at half his height; Selina offers to show Kent where he is
jokes.punchline: She roleplays a chipper, friendly Kent interaction ('Hi Kent, thanks for the memo!') then ends it with the most specific and emphatic possible dismissal: 'go and fuck yourself in your own asshole' — the 'in your own asshole' specification is the key to the joke
jokes.explanation: The structure is exceptional — starting with a fake-friendly roleplay then detonating into the insult makes the insult land harder. The specificity of 'in your own asshole' (as opposed to just 'go fuck yourself') is the key — why that specification? It's both more emphatic and somehow more humiliating. Selina performing cheerful professionalism while loading an insult into the scenario is peak character comedy. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Selina throws the lipstick at Kent and hits him in the eye
jokes.setup: Gary arrives with the replacement lipstick during a tense confrontation between Selina and Kent; Selina grabs it
jokes.punchline: Selina throws the lipstick — the object that has been the episode's running MacGuffin — and accidentally hits Kent in the eye during a political argument about her role
jokes.explanation: The lipstick running gag pays off in physical comedy. The MacGuffin that's been obsessed over all episode causes a genuine physical injury at the worst possible political moment. The escalation of the lipstick from missing → obsession → Amy's errand → Gary's panic → the actual throw is a textbook running gag payoff. The physical slapstick is the release valve for the episode's accumulated tension.

jokes.text: Fuck that lipstick!
jokes.setup: Gary tries to offer a different lipstick after Selina has just thrown one at Kent
jokes.punchline: After an entire episode of obsessing over the lipstick, Selina's 'Fuck that lipstick!' is the total repudiation of the running gag — the thing that consumed enormous energy is now worthless
jokes.explanation: Three words that close out the lipstick arc. The running gag is definitively over. The line works as a callback payoff — the thing everyone chased for 20 minutes gets dismissed in a profanity. Selina's complete reversal (from 'I need that lipstick' to 'fuck that lipstick') is both funny and character-revealing.

jokes.text: Kent. Oh, I'm so sorry about before. Although, God, you made a funny noise. I wish I could get that as my ringtone.
jokes.setup: Selina apologizes to Kent after accidentally hitting him in the eye with lipstick
jokes.punchline: The apology immediately pivots to describing the noise Kent made when hit as a desired ringtone — the most inappropriate possible expression of regret
jokes.explanation: A superb cringe beat. Selina begins the apology sincerely, then cannot stop herself from noting the comedic value of Kent's injury sound. Wanting it as a ringtone is the perfect level of inappropriate — not violent, just completely oblivious to the social moment. This is Selina's narcissism at its most lovable and most horrifying.

jokes.text: Just been with the president. He's asked me to stay on. My eye hurts.
jokes.setup: Everyone is worried about the situation with POTUS; Kent delivers news
jokes.punchline: Kent delivers two massive pieces of information (POTUS visited, Kent has been retained) in flat declarative sentences, then appends 'My eye hurts' with equal weight — treating his injury as bureaucratically equivalent to a presidential conversation
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of deadpan structure. The rhythm is everything: [major professional news] + [major professional news] + [minor physical complaint delivered identically]. The equal weight Kent gives 'my eye hurts' to presidential approval is character-perfect — he processes physical pain and political survival on the same affectless register. The three-beat structure is textbook and executed perfectly.

jokes.text: What's that shit all over the carpet? Oh, shit. Ooh. If you have some white wine vinegar, that'll get it right out.
jokes.setup: The lipstick has apparently smeared all over the carpet of what seems to be a significant room
jokes.punchline: Dan's immediate transition from political crisis mode to domestic cleaning advice — 'white wine vinegar will lift the stain' — is the bathos
jokes.explanation: Dan's domestic knowledge deployed in a political crisis is a good bathos beat. The specificity of 'white wine vinegar' is exactly right — Dan would know this.

jokes.text: What's that shit all over the carpet? / Oh, shit. / If you have some white wine vinegar, that'll get it right out.
jokes.setup: The lipstick has apparently been thrown/dropped and is staining the carpet of what appears to be the Oval Office area.
jokes.punchline: Dan — a political operative in the middle of a crisis in a sacred government space — offers a housekeeping tip ('white wine vinegar') as if this is a normal domestic situation.
jokes.explanation: The domesticity of 'white wine vinegar' in the context of a political catastrophe is funny. Dan's immediate pivot to stain advice is a perfect register collapse. Competent cringe comedy.

jokes.text: If you have some white wine vinegar, that'll get it right out.
jokes.setup: There's a stain on the Oval Office carpet from the lipstick incident
jokes.punchline: Selina — who just had a major political meltdown in the Oval Office — immediately transitions to practical stain-removal tips, as if the incident were simply a domestic accident
jokes.explanation: The pivot from explosive confrontation to household cleaning advice is very Selina — the practical homemaker emerging from the political operative. The specificity of 'white wine vinegar' (as opposed to any stain remover) grounds it in domestic authority that's completely incongruous with the Oval Office setting.

jokes.text: You take your eyebrows and you get out.
jokes.setup: Someone (presumably a staffer with notable eyebrows) offers unwanted help
jokes.punchline: The specific body part ('your eyebrows') as the thing being dismissed — not 'you get out' but 'your eyebrows get out' — suggests the eyebrows are somehow the most objectionable element of the person
jokes.explanation: The specific body part called out in the dismissal is the source of the joke's freshness. 'Take your eyebrows and get out' is a modification of the standard dismissal that requires some weird specificity — why eyebrows? The unexpectedness of it is funny. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You should not be in here. We need to go. I know. We'll do a little hop. Ready? One, two... there we go.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to leave the Oval Office but has stain on her shoe
jokes.punchline: The VP of the United States exits the Oval Office hopping on one shoe, counted out like a children's game
jokes.explanation: The VP hopping out of the Oval Office is strong physical comedy — the visual image is the joke. Gary's 'One, two... there we go' as a counting-in for the hop is the perfect attendant-to-principal moment: he's treating the VP like a child at a swimming lesson.

jokes.text: Feel like my lips have touched the veep's. It's quite a thrill. That's a little disrespectful.
jokes.setup: Amy arrives at Gary's girlfriend Dana's place to pick up the lipstick
jokes.punchline: Dana, holding the VP's lipstick, says she feels like she's kissed the VP vicariously. Amy's 'that's a little disrespectful' is the understated reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: A setup beat for the Amy/Dana scene. Dana's near-religious awe of the VP is funny but underdeveloped here.

jokes.text: Amy, I think we both know that any woman who's met him but didn't get to keep him is gonna see me as the enemy. I'm sorry, but you missed your chance.
jokes.setup: Dana implies Amy had romantic feelings for Gary and sees Dana as a rival
jokes.punchline: Dana positions herself as winning a competition Amy didn't know she was in — for Gary, who the audience understands to be a deeply absurd romantic prize. 'You missed your chance' with Gary as the prize is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the implication that Gary is a romantic prize worth competing for. The absurdity of Dana's confidence in Gary's desirability, when the audience has watched Gary obsess over lipstick all episode, makes the scene work.

jokes.text: So, Kim Jong whatever is swinging his nuclear dick again. The Russians have planted their flag on a Norwegian pile of pelican shit.
jokes.setup: Kent briefs Selina on her new foreign policy enhanced role
jokes.punchline: Kent's dismissive nicknames for two major international crises: 'Kim Jong whatever' (contempt for North Korea) and 'Norwegian pile of pelican shit' (contempt for the disputed Russian-Norwegian territory) reveal his clinical reductiveness about world affairs
jokes.explanation: Kent's geopolitical briefing language is exceptional. 'Kim Jong whatever' reduces a major nuclear threat to casual dismissal; 'Norwegian pile of pelican shit' is a vivid and specific piece of contemptuous geography. The character integration is near-perfect — Kent's coldness extends to treating nuclear crises as minor inconveniences. 'Swinging his nuclear dick' is also a strong image.

jokes.text: Uzbekistan is between Turkmenistan and I-could-give-a-fuck-istan. There's a map on page 376.
jokes.setup: Kent tells Selina about the Uzbekistan kidnapping crisis; she seems confused about where it is
jokes.punchline: Kent invents a neighboring country called 'I-could-give-a-fuck-istan' — a portmanteau dismissal — then follows up with the driest possible practical direction ('there's a map on page 376')
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. The invented country name is inventive wordplay — the '-istan' suffix carries both geographic accuracy and contempt. But the joke's true genius is the immediate pivot to 'there's a map on page 376' — the most mundanely practical possible follow-up to an obscene dismissal. It's structured like a teacher who just told a dirty joke and then says 'open your textbooks.' Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: POTUS wants you to do the morning shows. You will be the face of our failure.
jokes.setup: Selina has just been given an 'enhanced role' and thinks things are looking up
jokes.punchline: Kent reveals the enhanced role is being appointed the public face of electoral disaster. 'You will be the face of our failure' delivered flatly as if it's a promotion.
jokes.explanation: The line is perfectly constructed. 'You will be the face of our failure' is delivered with the same tone as 'you will be the face of our success' — the inversion is entirely in the content, not the delivery. Kent's clinical delivery makes it funnier than any ironic inflection would. The line also resolves the episode's central tension (Selina's fight for relevance) with the cruelest possible outcome: she gets relevance, but as a scapegoat.

jokes.text: I can't do that. I'm exhaustipated.
jokes.setup: Kent tells Selina she has 27 morning show interviews
jokes.punchline: Selina invents the word 'exhaustipated' — presumably a portmanteau of 'exhausted' and 'constipated' — as her medical excuse. The word is both funny in its construction and slightly disgusting in its implication.
jokes.explanation: A standout piece of wordplay. 'Exhaustipated' is Selina's invention — and it works because it genuinely sounds like a medical condition, it combines two real states, and it's slightly gross in a way that undercuts her dignity. Highly quotable and very character-specific to Selina's tendency to invent language under pressure.

jokes.text: We lost the battle, but we ain't cattle. Two years' time, bitches!
jokes.setup: Selina is about to face 27 morning show interviews as the face of electoral disaster
jokes.punchline: Her rallying cry is undercut by its absurdity — 'we ain't cattle' doesn't quite rhyme or make logical sense as a political metaphor; 'two years' time, bitches' is both aspirational and immediately self-undermining given she's about to go apologize on TV
jokes.explanation: Selina's self-motivating speeches are always a comedy source. 'We ain't cattle' is deliberately slightly wrong as a phrase — it almost works but doesn't quite. 'Two years' time, bitches' is funny for being both defiant and premature given the circumstances.

jokes.text: I'm exhaustipated.
jokes.setup: Selina is told she needs to do 27 morning show interviews
jokes.punchline: She describes her state as 'exhaustipated' — a portmanteau of exhausted and constipated implying she is so tired she can't even process what's happening
jokes.explanation: 'Exhaustipated' is a genuine coinage that combines exhaustion and constipation into a new word that somehow makes intuitive sense. One word doing significant comic work. Very quotable and economical — this is the kind of word invention that characterizes Selina's pattern of reaching for language when standard expressions fail her.

jokes.text: I need that stuff that junkies use. You know, when it takes a cop 15 bullets to put him down.
jokes.setup: Selina is exhausted before the morning shows and asks for stimulants
jokes.punchline: She escalates from asking for stimulants to describing PCP/bath salts — the 'takes 15 bullets' detail is both specific and darkly funny as a measure of desired alertness
jokes.explanation: Selina's extreme exhaustion expressed through a drug reference is character-appropriate and the specific '15 bullets' detail elevates it. The image of the VP requiring PCP to face morning television is both absurd and somehow relatable.

jokes.text: There's a horse coming out of my head.
jokes.setup: Selina is being prepared for the morning shows; she sees herself on a monitor and reacts
jokes.punchline: The pin she's wearing is positioned so that in the camera frame a horse appears to be emerging from her head — a classic poorly-composed background visual gag
jokes.explanation: A classic TV visual gag — object appearing to come out of someone's head in a shot. Works on its own but elevated by Selina's delivery ('There's a horse coming out of my head') which is perfectly flat. The horse pin is also a callback to Gary's earlier pin offer.

jokes.text: The White House wants to make sure that they are kept out of the veep's incident, so we want the lipstick throwing to have taken place here and not at the Oval Office.
jokes.setup: Dan is managing the political fallout from Selina hitting Kent with lipstick
jokes.punchline: The White House is already spinning the location of a lipstick-throwing incident — applying full political crisis management apparatus to an absurd domestic event
jokes.explanation: The political machine immediately processing a personal physical accident as a crisis to be managed is a perfect Veep satire beat. The precision of the location spin ('here, not at the Oval') applies Watergate-level damage control to cosmetics. Dan's matter-of-fact delivery makes it land.

jokes.text: Much like when Bigfoot got your mom pregnant, resulting in you.
jokes.setup: Dan says the lipstick incident was an accident; Selina disagrees with being called an accident
jokes.punchline: Selina's comeback traces Dan's origins to Bigfoot impregnating his mother — combining a cryptozoology joke with a mother insult
jokes.explanation: Selina's insult creativity is on display. 'Bigfoot got your mom pregnant' is a genuinely original insult — not just ugly but mythologically specific. The 'resulting in you' is the necessary button that makes it a proper insult rather than just a general statement about Bigfoot.

jokes.text: Not at all. I wouldn't say it's all bad news. Frank, no, I wouldn't say it was a shellacking. It's not a disaster. I mean, not for m-m... not for me... mean... excuse me.
jokes.setup: Selina is doing the morning shows claiming the loss isn't a disaster
jokes.punchline: She almost says 'not a disaster for me' — which is true, since she got her foreign policy role — but catches herself mid-sentence and loses the thread entirely, revealing both her personal self-interest and her verbal incapacity
jokes.explanation: Selina's near-gaffe is character-perfect — her first instinct is to defend herself personally ('not for me') but she catches it and spirals into verbal confusion. The 'm-m...not for me...mean...excuse me' transcription of her stumble is gold. The comedy is in watching her almost say the true thing live on television.

jokes.text: I'm about to enter a national ass-kicking contest with no legs and a massive ass. It's not that big, ma'am. What? I think your ass is perfect.
jokes.setup: Selina describes her situation metaphorically as entering an ass-kicking contest with no legs
jokes.punchline: Gary interprets 'massive ass' literally and tries to reassure Selina about her physical appearance; Selina's 'What?' reveals she meant it metaphorically; Gary continues with 'I think your ass is perfect' anyway
jokes.explanation: A superb Gary character joke. The structure is perfect: Selina's metaphor, Gary's literal interpretation, Selina's incredulous 'What?', and Gary's doubled-down physical compliment ('I think your ass is perfect'). The joke is both about Gary's literal-mindedness AND his devotion to Selina that makes him want to reassure her about anything she might feel bad about. His earnestness makes it funnier and sweeter than it has any right to be.

jokes.text: Want to play midterm cliché bingo? First phrase she uses. I already got 'wake-up call.'
jokes.setup: Selina's team watches her morning show appearances
jokes.punchline: Gary proposes midterm cliché bingo — a game about the predictability of political spin language. His immediate 'I already got wake-up call' before she's even used it suggests he knows her better than she knows herself
jokes.explanation: A sharp meta-commentary on political spin language. The bingo frame is a familiar conceit but well-deployed. Gary's immediate 'I already got wake-up call' is character-specific — he's studied her enough to predict her precisely.

jokes.text: It's not a disaster. I mean, not for m-m... not for me... mean... excuse me.
jokes.setup: Selina is doing a live interview insisting the results aren't a disaster.
jokes.punchline: She accidentally starts to say 'not for me' — implying she's personally fine even if everyone else isn't — then stumbles trying to correct herself live on air, making it worse.
jokes.explanation: A masterful live-TV gaffe moment. Selina accidentally reveals her genuine priority ('not for me' = I'm fine) mid-sentence on live TV, then stutters through an increasingly desperate correction that draws more attention to the slip. The m-m-m stumble is the cringe peak. Very Veep in that the character's actual thoughts keep escaping. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: The American people have said this is a wake-up call.
jokes.setup: Gary predicted 'wake-up call' for midterm cliché bingo
jokes.punchline: She uses it immediately, confirming Gary's prediction and completing the bingo joke
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff. Works as confirmation of Gary's prediction. Functional.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I look so old. No. What is that reaction?
jokes.setup: Selina watches herself on a monitor during the morning shows
